food_name: Putu Ayu (Steamed Pandan Coconut Sponge Kuih)

local_names.hindi: पुटु अयु (भाप में पकी पांडन नारियल केक)

aliases: Putu Ayu (Malay) — 'beautiful putu'; also 'kue putu ayu' (Indonesian)
पुटु अयु (भाप में पकी पांडन नारियल केक)

malay: Putu Ayu (Malay) — 'beautiful putu'; also 'kue putu ayu' (Indonesian)
hindi: पुटु अयु (भाप में पकी पांडन नारियल केक)

region_of_origin: Malay/Javanese origin; widely popular in Singapore and Malaysia
food_subgroup: Steamed traditional kuih — pandan coconut sponge in flower mould with fresh grated coconut topping; HIGH GL (25 per 4-piece serving)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for putu ayu specifically. Closest parent concept: steamed coconut-based traditional Southeast Asian sponge kuih. 'steamed cake' (FOODON:03315595) is the closest available parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for putu ayu. Closest parent category: traditional steamed cereal-based confection (A0012F).
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for putu ayu or equivalent traditional Southeast Asian steamed kuih.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of putu ayu is 4 pieces (approximately 110g), providing approximately 215 kcal, 37.4g carbohydrate, 3.9g protein, and 5.3g fat.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Putu Ayu / Steamed Pandan Coconut Kuih; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD traditional kuih entries
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) and Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (MyFCD)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Foodo Singapore database for steamed pandan coconut kuih and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD traditional kuih entries. Values represent a 4-piece (110g) serving scaled to per-100g basis. Minor micronutrient values (selenium, vitamin D, vitamin E, B-vitamins) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central analogous entries for egg and coconut milk components where HPB/MyFCD data were incomplete.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice Flour and Wheat Flour Blended Steamed Products — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for rice flour-based steamed cakes and mixed-flour confections; GI ~68 for rice flour/all-purpose flour blend steamed kuih
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for putu ayu in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~68 is estimated from the closest analogous entries: steamed rice flour-based cakes (GI ~70–72) adjusted downward marginally (~2–4 units) for the presence of all-purpose wheat flour, egg, and coconut milk, which modestly attenuate postprandial glucose response relative to pure rice flour products. GL 25 calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (68 × 37.4) / 100 ≈ 25.4, rounded to 25. This is classified HIGH GL (≥20). Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.guidance: Putu ayu is low in sodium (~60.5mg per 4-piece serving) and contains no added salt in the traditional batter, making it acceptable for hypertensive patients as an occasional snack; however, portion control remains important due to its moderate caloric density from coconut milk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL 25 per 4-piece serving) with GI ~68 and 37.4g available carbohydrate per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Putu ayu carries a HIGH glycemic load (GL 25 per 4-piece serving) and should be limited to 2 pieces (GL ~12, ~107 kcal) as an occasional treat for T2DM patients; pairing with a protein source and total carbohydrate counting at each snack occasion is strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (3.8g/100g, predominantly lauric acid) and egg-derived cholesterol (52mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat in putu ayu is primarily lauric acid from coconut milk, which has a more neutral cardiovascular profile than palmitic acid; nonetheless, patients with established cardiovascular disease should limit intake to 2 pieces per occasion and monitor overall saturated fat intake within daily dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Antioxidant micronutrients (selenium ~3.5mcg/100g, vitamin E ~0.4mg/100g, vitamin D ~0.2mcg/100g) from egg; lauric acid-dominant saturated fat is less pro-inflammatory than palmitic acid
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Putu ayu provides modest antioxidant micronutrients relevant to RA oxidative stress management and its coconut milk saturated fat is predominantly lauric acid (less pro-inflammatory than palmitic acid), making it acceptable in moderation for RA patients with no specific contraindication.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Putu ayu is a traditional Malay and Nonya steamed kuih of Javanese origin, widely sold at wet markets and hawker stalls across Singapore and Malaysia. Each piece is steamed in a small fluted flower-shaped mould: freshly grated coconut is pressed into the base of the mould, and a batter of rice flour, all-purpose flour, egg, sugar, coconut milk, and pandan juice is poured on top. When inverted after steaming, the grated coconut forms the visible decorative crown. The result is a soft, moist, lightly sweet sponge with a fragrant pandan aroma (from 2-acetyl-1-pyrroline), a delicate coconut flavour, and a slightly chewy grated coconut topping. Per 100g: 195 kcal, 3.5g protein, 4.8g fat (3.8g saturated, predominantly lauric acid from coconut milk), 34g carbohydrate, 12g sugar, 1.5g dietary fibre, 55mg sodium. A standard serving of 4 pieces (~110g) provides approximately 215 kcal, 37.4g carbohydrate, and a HIGH glycemic load of 25 (GI ~68). CONTAINS GLUTEN (all-purpose flour) and EGG — not suitable for coeliac or vegan patients. Halal, dairy-free, vegetarian. Clinically: HIGH GL makes this unsuitable for unrestricted T2DM consumption; limit to 2 pieces per occasion. Low sodium is favourable for hypertension. Steamed preparation (not fried) results in lower fat than baked equivalents. Sources: HPB Foodo Singapore, IMR Malaysia MyFCD.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Foodo Singapore and IMR Malaysia MyFCD — both approved primary regional sources for Singaporean and Malaysian dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (closest steamed rice flour-based cake entries) with documented estimation basis. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are populated from verified sources. Confidence: HIGH.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for putu ayu (rice flour + all-purpose flour + coconut milk + egg blend) are not separately reported in HPB Foodo or MyFCD as of 2026; USDA FoodData Central does not carry a direct putu ayu entry. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this traditional steamed kuih is not reported in HPB Foodo or MyFCD. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional putu ayu preparation (steamed, no margarine or shortening). GI ~68 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for putu ayu exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. GL 25 per 4-piece (110g) serving is classified HIGH (≥20). CONTAINS GLUTEN from all-purpose flour and EGG — not suitable for coeliac or vegan patients. Halal, dairy-free, vegetarian. Pandan aroma compound: 2-acetyl-1-pyrroline.
